regulator: Four parallel regulators cover this pillar, each scoped to its own geography: DFSA (Dubai Financial Services Authority, DIFC only); FSRA (Financial Services Regulatory Authority, ADGM only); CMA (Capital Market Authority, federal, UAE-wide outside the DIFC and ADGM financial free zones, successor to the Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) since 1 January 2026); VARA (Virtual Assets Regulatory Authority, Emirate of Dubai including its free zones, excluding the DIFC). The Dubai Land Department (DLD) also appears as an operational partner in VARA-scoped real-estate tokenisation, not as an independent financial regulator.
instruments: Under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC: DFSA Rulebook, Glossary Module (GLO 2), definitions of 'Investment' (GEN Rule A2.1.1), 'Investment Token' (a Token falling within the definition of an Investment -- a Security or a Derivative -- per GEN Rule A2.1.1(3)) and 'Security Token' (an Investment Token that is either a Security under GEN Rule A2.1.1(2)(b), or an instrument under GEN Rule A2.1.1(2)(c) conferring rights/obligations substantially similar to, or with a substantially similar purpose or effect to, a Security) -- both definitions confirmed by direct fetch of the DFSA's own rulebook pages, https://dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/investment-token and https://dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/security-token, each showing the entry as derived from DFSA RMI316/2021 (made 30 June 2021), version VER53/10-21; a Person carrying on a Financial Service relating to an Investment Token (operating a facility on which Investment Tokens are traded/cleared, advising on or arranging deals in them), making a Financial Promotion relating to one, or making an Offer to the Public of one, requires DFSA approval or authorisation

Under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC: DFSA Rulebook, Collective Investment Rules (CIR) Module, CIR 3.1.14 'Investment Token Fund' as a Specialist Class of Fund under CIR 3.1 -- confirmed by direct fetch of https://dfsaen.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/investment-token-funds, showing the current module version as CIR [VER40/01-26]; this research could not retrieve the full rule text of CIR 3.1.14 itself this session (see open_items)

Under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC: the DFSA's updated Crypto Token regulatory framework, in force 12 January 2026 with a reported three-month transition period, which replaced the DFSA's centrally-maintained 'Recognised Crypto Token' list with a firm-led self-assessment against DFSA-set suitability criteria, and which the DFSA's own announcement (title only, direct fetch of the page body blocked, see open_items) frames as applying to firms' crypto token business generally, with Investment Tokens (including Security Tokens) carved out of the ordinary Crypto Token definition as a separately-regulated category -- corroborated by secondary legal commentary (Bankslegal, Lexology) describing removal, in the same update, of the 30% cryptoasset exposure limit previously applied to Qualified Investor Funds

Under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM: Financial Services and Markets Regulations 2015 (FSMR), Section 58(2)(b), under which the FSRA deems a digital/virtual token that exhibits the economic and legal features and characteristics of a Security (as defined in FSMR Schedule 1, Part 3, Specified Investments) to be a 'Digital Security' and regulates it as a Security -- this test, including its 'tokenised offerings of Securities' framing and its case-by-case assessment approach, is confirmed by direct fetch of the FSRA's own page, https://www.adgm.com/setting-up/digital-assets ('Any digital token that exhibits the characteristics of a Security will be deemed and regulated as a Security by the FSRA'), corroborated by a search-engine-returned quotation of the ADGM Rulebook's own 'Regulatory Treatment Of Digital Securities' page (direct fetch of https://en.adgm.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/regulatory-treatment-digital-securities returned HTTP 403 this session, so that page's text is corroborated, not independently primary-fetched -- see open_items)

Under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM: Guidance -- Regulation of Digital Securities Activities in ADGM, issued under FSMR Section 15(2), current version identified as VER02.240220 -- its existence, legal basis, and current version number are confirmed via the FSRA's own adgm.com domain (search-indexed PDF at adgm.com/documents/legal-framework/guidance-and-policy/fsra/); direct fetch of the PDF itself returned HTTP 403 this session, so its detailed provisions are not independently quoted here (see open_items); market intermediaries and market operators dealing in or managing investments in Digital Securities must be licensed/approved by the FSRA as Financial Services Permission holders (including as Multilateral Trading Facilities), Recognised Investment Exchanges, or Recognised Clearing Houses, as applicable

Under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM): a dedicated Security Token / Commodity Token Contract regime, first published for consultation as a draft regulation by the then-Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) on 22 January 2025 (consultation closed 14 February 2025) -- confirmed by direct fetch of the SCA's own press release, which now 301-redirects to https://www.uaecma.gov.ae/en/media-center/news/22/1/2025/sca-invites-feedback-on-draft-regulations-for-security-tokens-and-commodity-tokens.aspx, corroborating that the SCA's own domain has migrated to uaecma.gov.ae following the SCA-to-CMA succession; the draft comprised 18 articles covering scope, definitions of security tokens and commodity tokens, technical standards, and rules on trading, settlement, pledging and cancellation; secondary legal commentary (Norton Rose Fulbright, Bird & Bird, Clyde & Co) reports the regulation was subsequently adopted around June 2025 as Decision No. 15/RM/2025 (also reported elsewhere as '15/Chairman/2025' -- the exact designation was not independently confirmed via a direct primary-text fetch this session, see open_items), requiring security/commodity tokens to be issued and traded only via an SCA/CMA-licensed Market or Alternative Trading System, with IPO-style prospectus disclosure for issuers, and reportedly excluding tokenised real-world assets from the security-token category unless the underlying asset is itself a security

Under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM): Federal Decree-Law No. (32) of 2025 concerning the Capital Market Authority and Federal Decree-Law No. (33) of 2025 concerning the Regulation of the Capital Market, both in effect since 1 January 2026, reconstituting the SCA as the CMA -- confirmed by direct fetch of https://www.uaecma.gov.ae/en/new-cma-law; secondary legal commentary (King & Spalding, Cleary Gottlieb) describes the CMA's mandate as expressly extending to digital assets and tokenised securities, but this research could not independently locate and fetch the specific statutory article making that extension (direct fetch of the Federal Decree-Law's own text via uaelegislation.gov.ae returned HTTP 403 this session -- see open_items), so that specific claim is not treated as independently primary-verified here

Under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC): VARA Virtual Asset Issuance Rulebook, current version dated 19 May 2025, effective from 19 June 2025 (prior versions: V.1, 7 February 2023; V.2, 19 September 2023; V.3, 1 October 2024) -- confirmed by direct fetch of https://rulebooks.vara.ae/rulebook/virtual-asset-issuance-rulebook; the rulebook defines Category 1 VA Issuance (Part I, Section D) to include Asset-Referenced Virtual Assets (ARVA, defined in Annex 2/Schedule 2), covering tokens backed by real estate, commodities or other real-world assets alongside Fiat-Referenced Virtual Assets (FRVA, Annex 1/Schedule 1), requiring issuers to hold a specific VARA licence, comply with applicable compulsory rulebooks, maintain reserve assets and secure prior VARA approval of a mandatory whitepaper (Part III, Schedule 1 -- VA Whitepaper Requirements); Category 2 VA Issuance (Part I, Section E) covers other tokens routed through VARA-licensed distributors/broker-dealers; and Exempt Virtual Assets (Part I, Section F) covers non-transferable and closed-loop tokens

Under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai (including its free zones, excluding the DIFC), operated jointly with the Dubai Land Department (DLD) as the mainland land registry: the DLD's Real Estate Tokenisation Project, launched as a pilot in March 2025 under the 'REES Real Estate Innovation Initiative' and expanded into a live secondary market for resale of tokenised real-estate interests from 20 February 2026 -- confirmed by direct fetch of the DLD's own primary announcement, https://dubailand.gov.ae/en/news-media/dubai-land-department-launches-phase-ii-of-the-real-estate-tokenisation-project-enabling-resale-in-the-secondary-market-from-20-february; that page names DLD and VARA as the two collaborating authorities and describes the project as operating 'within a regulated model' aligned with the Dubai Real Estate Sector Strategy 2033, but does not itself cite the Virtual Asset Issuance Rulebook or the ARVA classification by name -- secondary sources describe the tokens as falling under VARA's ARVA classification and as being linked to DLD-registered title deeds, denominated in dirhams, but that specific ARVA characterisation, and any operational role for the Central Bank of the UAE reported in some secondary coverage, was not confirmed via the DLD's own primary page this session (see open_items)
standing_summary: Tokenisation of real-world and financial assets in the UAE is not governed by a single national law but by four geographically separate regulatory regimes operating in parallel, each of which this research examined and confirmed independently: DFSA rules within the DIFC only; FSRA rules within ADGM only; the CMA's federal framework UAE-wide outside the DIFC and ADGM; and VARA's rules in the Emirate of Dubai including its free zones but excluding the DIFC. No sentence below should be read as describing a single 'UAE tokenisation law' -- there is none; each claim is scoped to the regulator that makes it. Under DFSA rules, applicable only within the DIFC, the operative mechanism is the 'Investment Token' concept, defined in the DFSA Rulebook's Glossary Module by reference to GEN Rule A2.1.1(3) as a Token falling within the definition of an Investment (a Security or a Derivative); a 'Security Token' is then a narrower Investment Token that either is a Security under GEN Rule A2.1.1(2)(b) or has rights/obligations or purpose/effect substantially similar to a Security -- both definitions were confirmed by direct fetch of the DFSA's own rulebook and trace to DFSA RMI316/2021 (made 30 June 2021). Carrying on a Financial Service relating to an Investment Token, promoting one, or offering one to the public within the DIFC requires DFSA approval or authorisation. The DFSA also maintains a dedicated fund vehicle for this asset class -- the Investment Token Fund, a Specialist Class of Fund under Collective Investment Rules (CIR) 3.1.14, current module version CIR [VER40/01-26] -- though this research could not retrieve CIR 3.1.14's substantive rule text this session. Separately, the DFSA's broader Crypto Token framework was updated with effect from 12 January 2026, moving from a centrally-maintained 'Recognised Crypto Token' list to firm-led self-assessment for ordinary Crypto Tokens; Investment Tokens (including Security Tokens) sit outside that ordinary Crypto Token definition as their own regulated category, and secondary commentary reports the same update removed a 30% cryptoasset exposure cap previously applied to DIFC Qualified Investor Funds -- this research could not directly fetch the DFSA's own announcement page for that update this session (it returned HTTP 403), so those specific details rest on secondary legal commentary, not an independent primary-text confirmation. Under FSRA rules, applicable only within ADGM, the mechanism is functionally similar but textually distinct: FSMR Section 58(2)(b) lets the FSRA deem any digital/virtual token exhibiting the economic and legal characteristics of a Security (as defined in FSMR Schedule 1, Part 3, Specified Investments) to be a 'Digital Security' and regulate it as an ordinary Security, on a case-by-case assessment basis -- confirmed by direct fetch of the FSRA's own adgm.com page, which states plainly that 'Any digital token that exhibits the characteristics of a Security will be deemed and regulated as a Security by the FSRA,' and corroborated by a search-indexed quotation of the ADGM Rulebook's own 'Regulatory Treatment Of Digital Securities' page (whose direct fetch was blocked this session). The FSRA has also issued dedicated Guidance -- Regulation of Digital Securities Activities in ADGM under FSMR Section 15(2) (current version VER02.240220 per adgm.com's own document index), though this research could not fetch that guidance document's body text this session (blocked with HTTP 403) and so does not quote its substantive content here. Market intermediaries and operators dealing in or managing Digital Securities in ADGM must be licensed or approved by the FSRA. Under the CMA's federal framework, applicable UAE-wide outside the financial free zones (DIFC and ADGM), the relevant instrument is a dedicated Security Token / Commodity Token Contract regime that the then-Securities and Commodities Authority (SCA) first published for public consultation on 22 January 2025 (consultation closed 14 February 2025) -- confirmed by direct fetch of the SCA's own press release, which now redirects permanently to the CMA's domain (uaecma.gov.ae), itself confirming the SCA-to-CMA succession described below. The 18-article draft covered the definition of security tokens and commodity tokens, technical standards, and rules on trading, settlement, pledging and cancellation of such tokens. Secondary legal commentary (not independently confirmed via a primary-text fetch of the final instrument this session) reports the regulation was adopted around June 2025 as Decision No. 15/RM/2025 (with at least one other secondary source citing it as '15/Chairman/2025' -- the discrepancy is unresolved here), requiring security and commodity tokens to be issued and traded only through an SCA/CMA-licensed Market or Alternative Trading System with IPO-style prospectus disclosure, and reportedly excluding tokenised real-world assets from the security-token category unless the underlying asset is itself a security (in which case oversight reportedly reverts to VARA or the relevant free-zone regulator instead). Separately, Federal Decree-Law No. (32) of 2025 (concerning the Capital Market Authority) and Federal Decree-Law No. (33) of 2025 (concerning the Regulation of the Capital Market) took effect 1 January 2026, reconstituting the SCA as the CMA as its legal successor -- confirmed by direct fetch of the CMA's own uaecma.gov.ae page -- and secondary commentary describes the CMA's resulting mandate as expressly extending to digital assets and tokenised securities, though this research could not independently fetch and quote the specific statutory article making that extension (the UAE's official legislation portal blocked direct fetch this session), so that particular claim is flagged as unconfirmed rather than asserted as independently primary-verified. Under VARA's rules, applicable in the Emirate of Dubai including its free zones but excluding the DIFC, the operative instrument is the Virtual Asset Issuance Rulebook, current version dated 19 May 2025 and effective from 19 June 2025 (following three earlier versions from February 2023 through October 2024) -- confirmed by direct fetch of VARA's own rulebooks.vara.ae site. The rulebook's Category 1 VA Issuance track covers Asset-Referenced Virtual Assets (ARVA), which the rulebook's own structure (an annexed schedule of definitions) extends to tokens backed by real estate, commodities or other real-world assets, alongside Fiat-Referenced Virtual Assets (FRVA) under the same Category 1 track; Category 1 issuers need a specific VARA licence, must maintain reserve assets, and must secure VARA's prior approval of a mandatory whitepaper. Category 2 covers other tokens distributed only through VARA-licensed intermediaries, and a narrow Exempt Virtual Assets category covers non-transferable or closed-loop tokens. This pillar's most concrete, primary-confirmed evidence of VARA's ARVA framework operating on genuine real-world assets is the Dubai Land Department's Real Estate Tokenisation Project, run jointly by DLD and VARA: a pilot launched March 2025 under the 'REES Real Estate Innovation Initiative,' followed by a live secondary market for resale of tokenised real-estate interests from 20 February 2026, confirmed by direct fetch of DLD's own primary announcement. That DLD page names DLD and VARA as the collaborating authorities and describes the project as operating within 'a regulated model,' but does not itself invoke the Virtual Asset Issuance Rulebook or the ARVA classification by name, or confirm a role for the Central Bank of the UAE that some secondary press coverage reports -- this research treats the precise regulatory characterisation of the DLD tokens (ARVA status, any CBUAE role) as corroborated by secondary sources only, not independently primary-confirmed, and flags it accordingly. Consistent with the September 2024 SCA-VARA coordination framework (also confirmed by direct fetch of the CMA/SCA's own press release, covering VASP licensing and mutual supervision generally rather than tokenisation specifically), VARA-licensed entities in Dubai obtain automatic federal registration for wider UAE reach, illustrating that these four regimes are coordinated at the margins even though, within this pillar, each regulator's tokenisation-specific rules remain textually and legally distinct and are reported here separately, never blended.

open_items: DFSA: this session could not directly fetch https://www.dfsa.ae/crypto or https://www.dfsa.ae/news/dfsa-issues-updated-rules-regulation-crypto-tokens-difc (both returned HTTP 403 on direct WebFetch). The description of the 12 January 2026 Crypto Token update -- the shift to firm-led self-assessment, the three-month transition, and the removal of the 30% cryptoasset exposure cap for Qualified Investor Funds -- rests on secondary legal commentary (Bankslegal, Lexology, CMS Law-Now) corroborating the DFSA's own (unfetched) announcement, not on an independently quoted primary text. This should be re-confirmed against the DFSA's own page before being treated as verbatim-verified.

DFSA: CIR 3.1.14's substantive rule text for 'Investment Token Fund' was not retrieved this session -- only its location (Collective Investment Rules Module, version VER40/01-26) was confirmed via direct fetch of the rulebook navigation page. The specific requirements a fund must meet to use this Specialist Class were not independently verified.

FSRA: direct fetch of https://en.adgm.thomsonreuters.com/rulebook/regulatory-treatment-digital-securities and of the Guidance -- Regulation of Digital Securities Activities in ADGM PDF (both hosted on FSRA-adjacent domains) returned HTTP 403 this session. The FSMR s.58(2)(b) / Schedule 1 Part 3 test is corroborated by a search-engine-returned quotation of the former page and by a direct fetch of adgm.com's own summary page, but the guidance document's substantive content (version VER02.240220) was not independently read or quoted this session.

CMA/SCA: the final adopted Security Token / Commodity Token Contract regulation -- reported in secondary commentary as Decision No. 15/RM/2025, adopted around June 2025, with at least one other secondary source citing it as '15/Chairman/2025' -- was not independently located and fetched from a CMA/SCA primary page this session, despite repeated attempts (uaecma.gov.ae's regulations-listing and latest-regulations pages returned only navigation shells, not a searchable document index, on direct fetch). Only the 22 January 2025 consultation-stage press release was directly primary-confirmed. The exact decision number, adoption date, and full article text should be re-verified against a CMA primary source before being treated as independently confirmed.

CMA/SCA: direct fetch of the Federal Decree-Law No. (33) of 2025 text via uaelegislation.gov.ae (both the direct legislation page and its /download PDF link) returned HTTP 403 this session. Secondary commentary's claim that the CMA's mandate 'expressly extends to digital assets and tokenised securities' is reported here as attributed commentary, not as an independently quoted statutory provision -- the specific article number and text should be re-confirmed.

VARA / Dubai Land Department: the DLD's own primary press release for the Phase II real-estate tokenisation launch (dubailand.gov.ae) names only DLD and VARA as collaborating authorities and does not itself invoke the ARVA classification or the Virtual Asset Issuance Rulebook by name, and does not mention any role for the Central Bank of the UAE. Secondary press coverage (Coindesk, Khaleej Times, Tokenizer) describes the tokens as VARA ARVA-classified, dirham-denominated, and linked to DLD title deeds, and some coverage adds a CBUAE role -- these characterisations are reported here as secondary-sourced, not primary-confirmed, and the CBUAE-role claim is deliberately omitted from the instruments/summary text above pending direct primary confirmation.

This pillar does not address the CMA's broader Virtual Assets Framework (five modules, eight regulated activities, announced 13 April 2026) in detail, since that framework's primary subject matter -- VASP/exchange, custody, dealing, advisory and portfolio-management licensing -- belongs to the Exchanges (VATP) and Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillars; it is cited here only as regulatory-succession context for the CMA's federal role.

This pillar does not address CBUAE-side stablecoin/payment-token regulation (Payment Token Services Regulation, Circular No. 2/2024) or the Digital Dirham CBDC programme, which are tracked in the Stablecoins and Banking & Money pillars respectively, not duplicated here.
